original_abstract: Rigid 1,3-disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) have emerged as essential linear bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings, often imparting superior pharmacokinetic properties to drug candidates. However, incorporating these motifs typically necessitates the use of [1.1.1]propellane—a volatile and unstable precursor—while existing alternatives often lack the broad reactivity of the parent hydrocarbon. In this work, we introduce stable thianthrenium-based BCP reagents designed for practical O-, N-, and C-alkylation. These reagents significantly expand the scope of bicyclopentylation, outperforming [1.1.1]propellane and other current benchmarks. By leveraging the unique redox and stereoelectronic characteristics of the thianthrene scaffold, we demonstrate the facile synthesis of these reagents via strain-release chemistry. Furthermore, the labile exocyclic C–S bond undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage upon single-electron reduction, generating BCP radicals that facilitate transition metal-mediated bond formations. The utility of this platform is showcased through the late-stage functionalization of complex molecules, maintaining high efficiency in the presence of diverse and sensitive functional groups.
humanized_abstract: Rigid 1,3-Disubstituted Bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) are widely used as linear bioisoster of para-substituted benzene ring and hence are prone to embed drug candidates with superior pharmacokinetic properties. In order to incorporate BCP structure, so far, the use of the volatile and unstable hydrocarbon, [1.1.1]propellane is inevitable. In addition, the alternatives which are synthesized through other routes do not possess comparable reactivity as that of the parent hydrocarbon. To this end, stable thianthrenium based BCP reagents for the O-, N-, and C-alkylation have been reported for the first time. These reagents are found to be better than the hydrocarbon [1.1.1]propellane and other existing reagents towards the bicyclopentylation reactions. Based on the redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene core, the reported strain-release method is employed for the synthesis of stable BCP reagents. The labile exocyclic C–S bond in the reagents undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage during single electron reduction of the thianthrenium core to generate the corresponding BCP radicals. These BCP radicals participate in the transition metal mediated bond forming reactions. Finally, late-stage functionalization of various complex structures by using these reagents has been demonstrated with high yields irrespective of diverse and sensitive functional groups present on the substrates.
